content:
Good. I now have enough sourced material to construct a comprehensive brief. Let me compile the findings.

TOP OF BOOK

  • ECB (MACRO) [DELTA ▲ — hawkish vs. prior path] — First ECB rate hike since 2023 delivered: +25bp on 11 June, DFR to 2.25% effective 17 June; inflation projections revised sharply higher.
    • Vs. baseline: The ECB raised interest rates by 25 basis points, the first increase since 2023, as policymakers emphasised their commitment to anchoring inflation at the 2% medium-term target. Hike itself was 100% priced — markets priced a 100.0% probability of a 25bps hike going into the meeting — so no price surprise on the decision itself. The material delta is in the projections and the absence of dovish guidance. The ECB revised inflation forecasts upward, now expecting headline inflation at 3.0% in 2026 (up from 2.6%) and 2.3% in 2027 (up from 2.0%); core inflation was also raised to 2.5% for both 2026 and 2027, from 2.3% and 2.2%. GDP was lowered to 0.8% in 2026 (from 0.9%) and 1.2% in 2027 (from 1.3%). Driver: the war in the Middle East is generating inflation pressures, and the decision is robust across a range of scenarios.
    • Guidance: Lagarde gave nothing away about the bank's likely next move in the press conference. The baseline remains a follow-up move in September, although a July hike cannot be ruled out. Markets currently expect at least one more rate hike this year.
    • Bank read-through: For European bank equities: DFR at 2.25% vs. the 2.00% floor since mid-2025 is NII-additive on the margin for liability-sensitive franchises and deposit re-pricing lag books (Nordics, Italian retail lenders). But GDP now projected at only 0.8% in 2026, a downward revision reflecting the war's impact on commodity markets, real incomes, and confidence — growth headwind that undermines loan-growth recovery thesis and is credit-negative for late-cycle borrowers. Net: NII tailwind, but credit-cost risk rising into H2. Stagflationary combination is specifically bad for banks with high CRE or leveraged-credit exposure. For funding: higher short rates push up bank wholesale funding costs; next TLTRO-equivalent is absent, so marginal funding cost of senior/preferred issuance rises. AT1 coupon resets on EURIBOR-linked instruments re-price upward (positive carry for holders), but spread compression from here is harder to justify if growth deteriorates. The ECB flagged that fiscal responses to the energy price shock should be temporary, targeted and tailored — signals no TPI backstop is imminent unless spreads dislocate materially.
    • ECB 12 June non-monetary decisions: Separately today, the ECB updated its structural financial indicators dataset, showing a further decline in EU bank offices of 2.62% — background structural data, not actionable.
    • Action: MONITOR next data prints (June flash HICP, July PMIs) for second-hike confirmation. On the bank equity book: nuanced — buy lagged re-pricer banks on dips, fade NII-sensitive names where growth risk is under-appreciated. On credit: AT1 carry remains compelling but spread tightening from here depends on path of next hike + growth trajectory.
    • Sources: ECB press release (11 Jun) | ECB press conference statement | ECB structural indicators (12 Jun)
  • UniCredit (UCG) / Commerzbank (CBK) [NEW — regulatory escalation] — BaFin formally drawn into tender dispute; acceptance now at ~10.95% but Commerzbank alleges near-zero genuine independent shareholder support.
    • Vs. baseline: Tender progress was known; what is new is the regulatory dimension and the granular attribution of tendered stock. Commerzbank stated no institutional investors have tendered shares; the acceptance level reached 10.95%; retail tendering remained at ~0.05%; and tendered shares stem almost exclusively from banks and parties connected to UniCredit that did not hold material Commerzbank stakes prior to the offer. Commerzbank also observed a more-than-tenfold increase in securities lending activity in its shares since the offer announcement.
    • Regulator angle: BaFin has been asked to examine whether the disclosure breached transparency rules; BaFin, which already barred UniCredit from publishing "inappropriate advertising" in April, is now weighing whether the tender announcement itself was misleading. A decision in Commerzbank's favour could further box UniCredit in, potentially requiring a correction or even a suspension of the offer's communications.
    • Key dates: The regular acceptance period expires midnight Frankfurt time on 16 June 2026, with preliminary results expected 19 June; a further acceptance window could open 20 June and run until 3 July.
    • Read: Acceptance rate of ~11% is arithmetically on track to push UCG above 30% direct stake only via derivative-linked entities, not independent shareholders — which is exactly Commerzbank's regulatory challenge. Throughout the acceptance period, Commerzbank's share price has traded significantly above the implied offer value; at market close 9 June it was approximately 6%, or €2.30 per share, above the implied offer price. Discount bid + no genuine institutional tender + BaFin probe = offer likely fails to gain majority control on independent shareholder votes. UCG is instead achieving its stated secondary goal: pushing its 27% Commerzbank stake just above the 30% mandatory threshold, allowing it to buy more shares on the market later. Equity leg: CBK stays bid above offer price — arb overhang limited; UCG equity not impacted materially. Credit leg: minimal spread impact either way at this stage; watch for regulatory overhang on UCG if BaFin sanctions. German government (12% CBK holder) remains opposed. Closing not before 2027.
    • Action: DIG IN on BaFin outcome next week. CBK: no new position on arb — discount bid means risk/reward is poor. UCG: monitor for any capital dilution risk from further CBK share acquisitions on market post-deadline.
    • Sources: Bloomberg (9 Jun) | Commerzbank press release | Investing.com

BY NAME / THEME

  • SpaceX (SPCX) IPO — European bank ECM read-through [IN LINE — event arrived] — Largest IPO in history priced at $135/share, $75bn raise, begins trading on Nasdaq today; European banks are named underwriters.
    • Vs. baseline: Pricing and today's listing are in line with guidance. The offering set a fixed offer price of $135.00 per share on 555.6 million Class A shares — a $75 billion raise valuing the company at approximately $1.77 trillion, surpassing Saudi Aramco's 2019 $29.4 billion record by more than 2.5×.
    • European bank read: Goldman Sachs, Morgan Stanley, BofA, Citi, JP Morgan, Barclays, Deutsche Bank Securities, RBC, UBS Investment Bank, and Wells Fargo serve as book-running managers; Societe Generale, ING, and Santander acted as co-managers. Fee pool: SpaceX is providing underwriters a gross spread of just 0.75% or less. Banks will split approximately $646 million in fees — more than twice the ~$300 million for Alibaba. For Barclays and Deutsche Bank (book-runners, regional distribution mandates for UK and continental Europe respectively), fee income is meaningful but at 0.75% gross the dollar quantum is spread across 23 banks. Net CIB P&L contribution is positive but not thesis-moving; the reputational/franchise value of the mandate is the primary gain. ING and SocGen as co-managers receive a smaller slice. No credit or capital structure implication.
    • Action: NO ACTION — fee income marginal at name level; equity impact immaterial. Watch secondary market performance for risk-appetite signal.
    • Sources: Investing.com | Fortune | SpaceXChart
  • ECB — Excess reserve remuneration change [IN LINE — previously decided, now taking effect] — From 17 June 2026 (next maintenance period), all excess reserves remunerated at the DFR regardless of where deposited.
    • Vs. baseline: Decided December 2025, formalised March 2026: excess reserves will be remunerated at the same rate — the deposit facility rate — whether deposited in the facility or held in reserve accounts. This is structural simplification, not a new policy signal. With DFR now at 2.25%, the change removes any incentive distortion in reserve placement. Minor administrative positive for banks with large reserve balances held outside the facility — negligible P&L delta at the margin.
    • Action: NO ACTION.
    • Sources: ECB non-monetary policy decisions (12 Jun)
  • Private Credit — European sector (background theme) [DELTA ▼ — sentiment shift building] — No single news event today, but the sector-level backdrop warrants a flag given the ECB macro shift.
    • Read: Blue Owl and Blackstone experienced substantial outflows early 2026 as nervous investors asked for their money back early. The ECB's stagflationary projection (growth down, inflation up) is a direct headwind: slower M&A activity reduces deal origination for direct lenders while rising rates push up borrower stress. A series of high-profile leveraged loan defaults in late 2025 and rising use of payment-in-kind toggles in direct lending point to mounting stress. European bank CEOs publicly downplay contagion risk (senior leaders signalled confidence in their institutions' exposure and downplayed the likelihood of major risks), but a poll of participants at Morgan Stanley's European Financials Conference shows private credit is seen as the top short-term risk. The divergence between management reassurance and institutional risk perception warrants watch. Watch for any NAV markdown disclosures or redemption gating events in European BDC/semi-liquid structures.
    • Action: MONITOR — no new specific event, but the ECB macro shift (higher-for-longer, weaker growth) tightens the credit cycle timeline. Flag any Q2 NAV marks or redemption events when published.
    • Sources: S&P Global | Morgan Stanley

CREDIT CORNER

  • Bund / Peripheral Spreads — post-ECB hike [IN LINE] — Germany's 10-year Bund yields remained steady just above 3.05%, close to three-week highs, following the ECB's expected 25bp rate increase. BTP-Bund spread
